summary: DHH argues that AI's creative development follows the same trajectory as human artists and national economies: starting with poor copies, progressing to good copies, then achieving originality. He draws parallels to Japan's and China's economic rise through imitation, and to how artists learn by copying masters. He contends that AI is currently in the 'good copy' phase and that true AI creativity is inevitable. Critics fixating on minor flaws in AI-generated art, like the Ghibli trend, will only accelerate the push toward genuine originality.
key_points: All creativity builds on imitation — learning to copy well is the direct path to mastery, not a detour
Japan and China followed the same copy-to-original arc economically that AI is now following creatively
AI has already passed the 'cheap copy' phase and is firmly in 'good copy' territory, with originality likely imminent
Whether AI creativity is desirable is a moral question, but it's going to happen regardless
Critics nitpicking AI's current flaws are inadvertently motivating AI builders to push past imitation faster
money_quotes: Stealing good ideas isn't a detour on the way to becoming a master — it's the straight route. And it's nothing to be ashamed of.
You start with a cheap copy, then you learn how to make a good copy, and then you don't need to copy at all.
AI has sped through the phase of cheap copies. It's now firmly established in the realm of good copies. You're a fool if you don't believe originality is a likely next step.
Whether we want AI to become truly creative is a fair question — albeit a theoretical or, at best, moral one. Because it's going to happen if it can happen, and it almost certainly can.
AI builders, like the Japanese and Chinese economies before them, eager to demonstrate an ability to exceed.
All that is to say that AI is in the 'Good Copy' phase of its creative evolution. Expect 'The Great Artist' to emerge at any moment.
key_insight: AI's creative trajectory mirrors how artists and economies have always evolved — from cheap imitation to good imitation to originality — and the leap to genuine AI creativity is now imminent and unstoppable.
